evals.prompt: I need to add a webhook retry system to our notification service. When a webhook delivery fails, it should retry with exponential backoff up to 5 times. We're using Python with FastAPI and storing webhook configs in DynamoDB. Plan this out for me.
evals.expected_output: A comprehensive feature plan with Definition of Done, pseudocode implementation, test specifications, and architectural review covering all five lenses.
expectations: Plan starts with a Definition of Done section containing specific, observable completion criteria (not vague statements like 'works correctly')
Definition of Done includes acceptance scenarios with Given/When/Then format using concrete values (e.g., specific retry intervals, HTTP status codes)
Plan includes an Out of Scope section explicitly listing what is NOT included
Plan includes unit test specifications written BEFORE implementation with concrete input values and expected outputs
Test specifications include happy path, edge cases, error cases, AND error propagation tests
Plan uses pseudocode (not full implementation code) to describe the approach
Plan includes setup instructions referencing git worktree creation via ~/scripts/wt.sh
Plan includes an Architectural Review section covering all 5 lenses (Scalability, Reliability, Cost, Fitness for Purpose, Code Health)
Plan includes observability section with structured logging, correlation IDs, and metrics
Plan includes explicit trade-offs section documenting architectural decisions with rationale
Plan references quality checks appropriate to the stack (ruff, mypy, bandit for Python)
Plan includes an Autonomous Execution Safety Checklist section with all items checked and no vague language remaining

evals.prompt: We need to build a CSV export feature for our admin dashboard. Users should be able to export filtered data from any table view. The frontend is React/TypeScript and the backend is a Node.js Express API with PostgreSQL. How should we approach this?
evals.expected_output: A detailed feature plan that handles the non-Python stack appropriately, with Definition of Done, pseudocode, tests, and architectural review.
expectations: Plan starts with a Definition of Done section with specific, testable criteria
Definition of Done criteria describe observable behavior (e.g., 'clicking Export CSV downloads a file containing all visible rows with applied filters')
Plan includes acceptance scenarios with concrete values (specific filter combinations, expected row counts, column headers)
Plan adapts quality check recommendations to the Node.js/TypeScript stack (not defaulting to Python tools like ruff/mypy)
Plan includes test specifications with concrete input values and expected outputs
Plan uses pseudocode to show the approach, not full TypeScript/React code
Plan addresses scalability — what happens with large datasets (pagination, streaming, background jobs)
Plan includes an architectural review covering reliability (what if the export fails mid-stream?)
Plan includes a Fitness for Purpose review (streaming vs buffered, CSV library choice)
Plan includes error propagation tests — verifying errors are not silently swallowed

evals.prompt: I want to add rate limiting to our public API. We need per-customer limits with different tiers (free: 100/hour, pro: 1000/hour, enterprise: 10000/hour). The API runs on AWS Lambda behind API Gateway. Let's think through how to build this.
evals.expected_output: A comprehensive plan covering the rate limiting implementation with Definition of Done, pseudocode, test specs, and thorough architectural review especially around scalability and cost.
expectations: Plan starts with a Definition of Done containing specific criteria for each tier's behavior
Acceptance scenarios include concrete rate limit values and expected responses when limits are exceeded (specific HTTP status codes, response bodies, retry-after headers)
Plan includes test specifications for boundary conditions (exactly at limit, one over limit, limit reset timing)
Plan addresses the architectural challenge of distributed rate limiting across Lambda instances
Cost optimization review addresses the storage/lookup cost of rate limit counters at scale
Plan includes reliability analysis — what happens if the rate limit store (Redis/DynamoDB) is unavailable
Plan uses pseudocode to describe the rate limiting algorithm
Plan includes Code Health review — checks for existing anti-patterns in the API code
Plan includes observability section with metrics for rate limit hits and rate limit exceeded counts
Plan includes an Autonomous Execution Safety Checklist section with all items checked and no vague language remaining
